title: End-of-Lease Cleaning for Commercial Tenants in Newcastle — What You Actually Need to Do
excerpt: Your lease is ending and you need the premises back to its original condition — but most commercial tenants in Newcastle underestimate what that actually involves. Here's what you need to know before you hand back the keys.
content:
Your lease is ending. You're excited about the new space. The last thing you want to deal with is a dispute over cleaning.

But end-of-lease cleaning is one of the most common flashpoints between commercial tenants and landlords in Newcastle — and it costs tenants money they didn't budget for. Here's what you're actually required to do, and how to make sure you get your bond back and leave on good terms.

## What Does Your Lease Actually Say?

Before you do anything else, dig out your lease and read the make-good clause.

Commercial leases in NSW typically require you to return the premises in the same condition it was in when you moved in — fair wear and tear excepted. What that means in practice varies, but cleaning is almost always part of it.

Some leases are vague. Some are extremely specific — professional carpet steam cleaning, external window cleaning, detailed kitchen degreasing. If your lease is specific, that's your checklist. If it's vague, you still need to meet a standard that would hold up if the landlord disputed the condition.

Don't assume a standard office clean will cut it. In most cases, it won't.

## What "Make Good" Looks Like from a Cleaning Perspective

Think of it in two layers: routine cleaning and periodic cleaning — and for end-of-lease, you need both.

**Routine cleaning** covers the basics:
- All hard floors vacuumed, mopped, and polished if applicable
- All surfaces wiped down including desks, shelves, windowsills
- Kitchens cleaned including inside cupboards, appliances, and rangehood filters
- Bathrooms and toilets cleaned and sanitised
- Bins emptied and cleaned
- Light switches, door handles, skirting boards wiped

**Periodic cleaning** is where most tenants fall short:
- Carpet steam cleaned (most leases require this regardless of condition)
- Windows cleaned inside and out
- Blinds cleaned or replaced if stained
- Air conditioning vents and filters cleaned
- High dusting — light fittings, fans, vents above head height
- Walls wiped down for marks and scuffs

A lot of tenants run a mop over the floors, give the kitchen a wipe, and hand back the keys. That's not going to hold up against a proper inspection.

## The Ingoing Condition Report Is Your Best Friend

If you had an ingoing condition report done at the start of your lease — photos, written notes, signed by both parties — that's your benchmark. You need to get back to that standard.

If you didn't have one (and plenty of tenants don't), you're at a disadvantage. Without it, the baseline is essentially the landlord's recollection versus yours. That's not a good position to be in.

If you're reading this before your lease ends and you haven't started a condition report, do it now. Even a dated photo record of how the space looks today is worth having.

## Timing Matters — Don't Leave It to the Last Day

End-of-lease cleaning takes longer than people expect, especially in commercial spaces. A 200sqm office in Wickham or a shopfront in Charlestown that's had two years of daily use is going to take a full day's work to bring up to standard — probably more if carpets and windows are involved.

Book your cleaners a few days before your actual vacate date. That gives you time to:
- Walk through the cleaned space before the landlord inspection
- Fix anything that got missed
- Request a re-clean on specific areas without the pressure of a same-day deadline

Leaving it to the last day is the fastest way to end up with a dispute you could have avoided.

## Who Does the Cleaning?

You can use your existing commercial cleaner if they offer end-of-lease services — but check that they do. Not all routine commercial cleaners are equipped for a full make-good clean. It requires different equipment, more time, and a different checklist.

Get a written scope of work before you confirm anyone. That means an itemised list of exactly what will be cleaned, what products will be used on carpets, and whether windows are included. If it's not in writing, it's not agreed.

Also — make sure they're insured. Working in a space you no longer occupy, with the landlord about to inspect, is not the time to discover someone isn't covered.

## What Happens If You Don't Do It Properly?

The landlord gets to arrange their own cleaners and bill you for it — usually at a higher rate than if you'd organised it yourself. They can deduct it from your bond. If it exceeds the bond, they can pursue you for the balance.

Beyond the money, it damages the relationship. Commercial property in Newcastle is a small world. If you're planning to lease again — or use the same agent or landlord — leaving a mess is something people remember.

## Make It Simple

If you're vacating commercial premises in Newcastle and want to know exactly what's needed to meet your make-good obligations — or you want one team to handle the whole thing — talk to YOS Cleaning.
